title: Patching a small plasterboard hole
subtitle: The everyday wall-repair job
excerpt: A step-by-step technique for filling a hole in plasterboard up to about the size of a 50p piece, the common picture-hook-came-out, doorknob-went-through-the-wall, the-radiator-tee-was-rerouted kind. Fill, feather, sand, paint. Two evenings end-to-end.
sourceNotes: Method is the standard one-coat-filler-and-feather technique taught in the City & Guilds Painting and Decorating syllabus and in the long-running UK trade press. Newey & Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (c. 1905) for the underlying principle of feathering a patched surface into the surrounding wall. Modern lightweight filler chemistry post-dates the PD canon, the technique is the same as for traditional putty repairs; the material is more forgiving.

content.text: Sooner or later a wall takes a small hole. A picture hook comes out clean, leaving a 4 mm bruise. A doorknob bashes through and leaves a 30 mm crater. A plumber re-routes a radiator tee and leaves a tidy 50 mm square cut. The technique for all three is the same: clean the hole, fill, feather, sand, paint. This article is the method; the variations sit at the end.

The job runs across two evenings, twenty minutes the first, ten the second, because the filler needs an overnight to cure before sanding. Rushing the cure is the most common cause of a patch that shows through the paint.
What you'll need, materials
Lightweight
one-coat filler

, Polyfilla One-Time, Tetrion Decorators Filler, or any general builders'-merchant brand. The 600 g tub is plenty for ten small holes. Ready-mixed is more forgiving than powder-mix for one-evening work.
Matching paint for the final coat. The leftover tin from the wall's last decoration is best; a small tester pot of the same colour is the backstop.
What you'll need, tools
50 mm filling knife.
For pressing filler into the hole and dragging the first stroke across the surface.
100 mm filling knife.
For
feathering
the edges of the patch into the surrounding wall. The wider blade lets you tail the filler down to zero across a hand's-width.
Stanley knife.
For undercutting the inside of the hole, see step 1.
Sanding block with 180-grit sandpaper.
For levelling the cured patch. A folded quarter-sheet held in the hand will do at a pinch but leaves an uneven surface.
A cotton dust sheet
(optional). Filler dust travels further than you think. A 1.5 × 1 m piece under the working position catches the bulk.
Step by step, evening one
Step 1, Clean and undercut the hole.

Pull any loose paper or filler out of the hole with the corner of the Stanley knife. If the hole has clean edges, run the knife around the inside at a slight outward angle, the hole should be very slightly wider at the back of the wall than at the front. This undercut gives the filler something to lock into; a perfectly cylindrical hole pops the filler out within a year as the wall flexes. Three or four passes is enough.
Step 2, Mix or prepare the filler.

Ready-mixed filler comes ready. Powder-mix filler needs a small bowl, a splash of water, and a stir to thick custard, thinner than peanut butter, thicker than yoghurt. Mix only what you'll use in the next ten minutes; the cure starts the moment water meets powder.
Step 3, Press filler into the hole.

Load the 50 mm filling knife with a walnut-sized blob of filler. Push the blade into the hole at a low angle, pressing filler against the back wall first so there are no air pockets. Then drag the knife across the surface, leaving the filler proud of the wall by a millimetre. Don't try to leave it flush, the cure shrinks the filler very slightly, and the sanding step in the morning takes the proud millimetre off.
Step 4, Feather the edges.

Swap to the 100 mm filling knife. Hold the blade nearly flat against the wall and draw it outward from the centre of the patch. The filler should taper from proud at the patch to zero a hand's-width away. Work in four strokes, up, down, left, right. The visible edge of the patch should now be a soft transition, not a step.
Step 5, Walk away.

Lightweight filler is touch-dry inside two hours but does not sand cleanly until it has cured all the way through. Leave it overnight. In a cold or damp room, leave it a full 24 hours. The label has the cure schedule; trust the label.
Step by step, evening two
Step 6, Sand the patch level.

Wrap a quarter-sheet of 180-grit sandpaper around the sanding block. Sand with light pressure in a circular motion, working outward from the centre. The proud millimetre comes off in a minute. Stop the moment your fingers cannot feel the edge of the patch when you run them across the wall with your eyes closed. Over-sanding scoops the filler back below the wall level, frustrating, because the only fix is to fill again.
Step 7, Wipe and prime.

Dust the wall with a soft brush or a slightly damp cloth. New filler is more absorbent than old paint; if you skip a primer coat, the patch shows through the topcoat as a slightly flatter sheen. Brush a thin coat of matching paint over the patch and the feathered area, and let it dry. Two hours is enough for emulsion.
Step 8, Paint the topcoat.

Roll or brush a topcoat across the patched area, blending into the surrounding wall by two hand's-widths in every direction. If the wall has a roller texture, use the same roller and the same pressure, a brushed patch in a roller-finished wall reads as a smooth shadow under raking light. Two coats are usually needed for a perfect match; the second goes on after the first is dry.
What it looks like when it's right

Stand back from the patched wall under whatever lighting the room normally has, and look across the patch from each side. A successful patch is invisible from anywhere in the room. Under raking side light, a low winter sun, a side-mounted lamp at evening, the patch may still show as a softer matt area; this means the topcoat hasn't fully cured, and the sheen will even out over a few days.
Variations
Hole bigger than a 50p piece.
Filler alone won't bridge a hole bigger than about 50 mm. It sinks as it sets. A self-adhesive aluminium-mesh
plasterboard

patch sticks across the hole as a bridge, and the filler is applied over the top. Holes bigger than 150 mm need a cut-and-patch with a fresh piece of plasterboard, a different job, treated in its own tutorial.
Damp wall (kitchen, bathroom).
Use a powder-mix filler rated for high-moisture environments rather than the standard lightweight version. The cure schedule is the same; the cured filler resists water absorption.
Deep hole (more than 15 mm front-to-back).

Fill in two layers rather than one. The first layer fills to within 5 mm of the surface; let it cure overnight; the second layer brings it proud, feathers, and sands the next evening. Trying to fill a deep hole in one go leaves a soft uncured core that cracks under the paint within months.
Care

A correctly filled and painted patch lasts as long as the wall behind it. The two things that put a patch at risk are damp (re-occurring water tracks through a cured filler), and house movement (cracks open at the edge of a patch faster than across an unpatched wall). Re-paint the wall on the same schedule as the rest of the room, usually every four to six years for emulsion.
